Key Points of the Case: Coronary artery disease, triple vessel disease  

[Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint:  
Intermittent precordial pain for 18 years.  
Present Illness History:  
The patient experienced precordial pain after exertion 18 years ago, described as squeezing, accompanied by cold sweat, without radiation to the shoulder or back, dizziness, hemoptysis, or palpitations, lasting about 3-5 minutes, relieved by rest. He sought treatment at a local hospital, diagnosed with "coronary heart disease," and was prescribed oral secondary prevention medications for coronary heart disease. Subsequently, the symptoms recurred intermittently, relieved by rest and oral "fast-acting heart-saving pills." Three years ago, he was hospitalized for "acute myocardial infarction" at a local hospital for intravenous treatment (details unclear). One week ago, the symptoms recurred, and coronary angiography at a local hospital showed: coronary heart disease, triple vessel disease, with approximately 90% stenosis in the proximal left anterior descending artery. He came to our hospital for surgical treatment and was admitted to our department with a diagnosis of "coronary heart disease, triple vessel disease." Since the onset of the illness, his mental state and appetite have been good, and bowel and bladder functions are normal.  
Past Medical History:

    "name": "Serum Creatinine", "claim_value": "6.17 mg/dL",
    "evidence_value": "6.17 mg/dL", "correct": correct_scr
})

    "name": "Age", "claim_value": "58 years",
    "evidence_value": "66 years", "correct": correct_age
})

correct_gender = (claim_gender == evidence_gender)
entities.append({
    "name": "Gender", "claim_value": "Female",
    "evidence_value": "Male", "correct": correct_gender
})

# ── STEP 2: calculate using evidence values ─────────────────────────────────
# Formula: GFR (mL/min/1.73 m²) = 142 x (Scr/A)^B x 0.9938^age x gender_coefficient
# where A and B depend on gender and Scr
if evidence_gender == "Female":
    if evidence_scr <= 0.7:
        A = 0.7
        B = -0.241
    else:
        A = 0.7
        B = -1.2
    gender_coefficient = 1.012
else:
    if evidence_scr <= 0.9:
        A = 0.9
        B = -0.302
    else:
        A = 0.9
        B = -1.2
    gender_coefficient = 1

formula = "GFR (mL/min/1.73 m²) = 142 x (Scr/A)^B x 0.9938^age x gender_coefficient"
substitution = f"142 x ({evidence_scr}/{A})^{B} x 0.9938^{evidence_age} x {gender_coefficient}"

# Calculate GFR

claimed_result_val = 8.0702  # last claim segment

# Accuracy criterion — equation-based → within 5 %

else:

    "correct":         calc_correct,
}

# ── STEP 3: derive label ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Treatment Outcome: Condition improved
Key Points of the Case: Rheumatic heart disease

Intermittent chest tightness for 1 year, worsened for 13 days
Present Illness History:
The patient experienced chest tightness after exertion 1 year ago, without chest pain, throat constriction, sweating, shortness of breath, palpitations, dizziness, blurred vision, nausea, or vomiting, lasting 10 minutes and resolving spontaneously. Since then, intermittent episodes of the same symptoms occurred, mostly after activity, each lasting 10 minutes and resolving, which the patient did not take seriously. 13 days ago, at 10 PM while preparing for bed, the patient experienced chest tightness and chest pain, accompanied by sweating, without shortness of breath, throat constriction, palpitations, dizziness, blurred vision, nausea, or vomiting, lasting 1 hour and resolving. Since then, there has been one episode daily, with the same nature, mostly occurring before sleep, and in severe cases, the patient self-administered "fast-acting heart-saving pills 3-5 tablets," which resolved after 10 minutes. A month ago, the patient visited the First People's Hospital of the city, diagnosed with "rheumatic heart disease with mild mitral stenosis and moderate regurgitation, moderate to severe aortic stenosis with mild to moderate regurgitation, severe tricuspid regurgitation, left and right atrial enlargement, heart function class IV (NYHA classification), arrhythmia, atrial fibrillation, third-degree atrioventricular block, grade 3 hypertension (very high risk), type 2 diabetes, diabetic foot, thrombocytopenia, herpes zoster, benign prostatic hyperplasia," and was given symptomatic treatment, with a recommendation for early heart valve replacement surgery and permanent pacemaker implantation, but the patient refused heart valve replacement surgery. Currently, the patient is admitted to our department for pacemaker implantation. Since the onset of the illness, the patient has had good spirits, appetite, and sleep, with bowel movements 1-2 times daily, unformed, difficulty urinating, taking "finasteride, tamsulosin," with no significant change in weight.
Past Medical History:
Diagnosed with "rheumatic heart disease, mitral stenosis" 32 years ago, without significant symptoms and no treatment. Persistent atrial fibrillation was discovered 6 years ago, without medication. Hypertension for 6 years, with a maximum of 120/70 mmHg, taking "lacidipine 4 mg once daily; sacubitril/valsartan 25 mg twice daily," with blood pressure controlled at 110/50 mmHg. Diabetes history for 6 years, taking "acarbose 50 mg three times daily," without blood glucose monitoring. Chest: Chest shape normal, symmetrical, chest wall veins engorged, no subcutaneous emphysema, no tenderness on the chest wall, intercostal spaces normal, both breasts symmetrical, no nipple retraction, no nipple discharge, no masses in both breasts.
Lungs: Inspection: Bilateral lung respiratory movements consistent, breathing rhythm even. Palpation: Vocal fremitus normal, no pleural friction felt. Percussion: Bilateral lung percussion shows clear sound. Auscultation: Bilateral lung breath sounds diminished, bilateral lung moist rales audible, no significant dry rales, vocal resonance not diminished, no pleural friction sounds heard.
Heart: Inspection: No bulging in the precordial area, no abnormal pulsations, apex beat normal. The strongest point of apex beat is located in the 5th intercostal space, 0.5 cm medial to the midclavicular line, bowel sounds normal, bowel sounds 3-5 times/min. Anus, rectum, and external genitalia not examined. Physiological curvature of the spine present, no tenderness in the spine, limbs move freely, no deformities, edema in the lumbosacral region, no swelling in both lower limbs, muscle strength in both upper limbs 4/5, muscle strength in both lower limbs 2/5. Joints without redness or swelling, no tenderness, dorsalis pedis pulses present bilaterally, sensation, pain, pressure, and position sense in both feet symmetrical and normal. Bilateral physiological reflexes normal, no pathological reflexes elicited.
Auxiliary Examinations:
X-ray: "Mitral valve" type heart
Bilateral lower limb ultrasound: Left popliteal vein thrombosis with partial recanalization (subacute), left calf intermuscular vein thrombosis with partial recanalization (subacute), left great saphenous vein reflux, no significant abnormalities in bilateral lower limb arteries.

Holter ECG: Average heart rate 41 bpm, analyzed heartbeats 58,458. Longest RR interval 1.718 seconds. No atrial fibrillation during monitoring, accompanied by slow ventricular rate, occasional multifocal ventricular premature beats, presenting as a short episode of ventricular tachycardia (composed of 4 heartbeats), intermittent third-degree atrioventricular block with junctional escape rhythm, ST-T changes.
Electrocardiogram: Atrial fibrillation with third-degree atrioventricular block.
Complete Blood Count: White blood cell count 3.3×10^9/L, red blood cell count 3.74×10^12/L, neutrophil percentage 30.44%, lymphocyte percentage 41.14%, monocyte percentage 23.34%, eosinophil percentage 1.54%, basophil percentage 3.74%. Hemoglobin 121 g/L, platelet count 85×10^9/L.
Cardiac Biomarkers: NT-proBNP: 4043 pg/ml, creatine kinase 83 U/L, creatine kinase isoenzyme 9 U/L, serum troponin I 0.01 ng/ml. The patient has a history of smoking.

[Case Introduction]
Chief Complaint:
Coma for 3 hours, vomiting once.
Present Illness:
The patient's family reported that the patient was taking a shower at home around 3 PM today, with the gas cylinder outside the bathroom, and the doors and windows closed. At around 6 PM, the family found the patient collapsed on the floor, unresponsive to calls, with no medications or food nearby, no vomiting, no seizures, and no incontinence. They urgently called 120, and the patient was transported to our hospital by ambulance. During transport, the patient vomited once, expelling gastric contents without coffee-colored liquid. Upon admission, the patient was in a coma and had not urinated or defecated.
Past Medical History:
Denies history of "hypertension," "diabetes," "coronary heart disease," etc., denies history of infectious diseases such as "hepatitis," "tuberculosis," no history of drug or food allergies, no history of trauma or surgery. No history of blood transfusion. Vaccination history is unclear.
Physical Examination:
T: 36.1℃, P: 78 beats/min, R: 21 breaths/min, BP: 108/66 mmHg. Development is normal, nutritional status is moderate, in a comatose state, transported into the ward on a stretcher, somewhat uncooperative. No jaundice or petechiae on the skin and mucous membranes, no enlargement of superficial lymph nodes. No deformities of the skull, no pallor of the conjunctiva, pupils equal and round, approximately 3.0 mm in diameter, responsive to light, lips slightly pale, tongue in the midline, no congestion of the pharynx, tonsils not enlarged, neck soft with no resistance, trachea midline, thyroid not enlarged. Chest symmetrical, no deformities, intercostal spaces not widened, symmetrical respiratory movements bilaterally, normal tactile fremitus, percussion of both lungs shows resonance, clear breath sounds bilaterally, no dry or wet rales heard. No bulging in the precordial area, apex beat not diffuse, located 0.5 cm medial to the midclavicular line in the left 5th intercostal space, no thrill, heart borders not enlarged on percussion, heart rate 78 beats/min, regular rhythm, heart sounds strong, no pathological murmurs heard in the auscultation areas of the heart valves. Abdomen flat, no visible gastrointestinal peristalsis or waves, no varicosities in abdominal wall veins, no surgical scars, abdomen soft, liver and spleen not palpable below the ribs, shifting dullness negative, bowel sounds 4 times/min. **External genitalia normal, spine shows physiological curvature, no deformities in limbs, no edema in lower limbs. Pathological reflexes not elicited.
Auxiliary Examinations:
Emergency cranial CT showed no significant abnormalities. Electrocardiogram showed: sinus rhythm, 76 beats/min, normal ECG. Random peripheral blood glucose 6.8 mmol/L. Blood analysis: white blood cells 7.2*10^9/L, neutrophil percentage 63.2%, lymphocyte percentage 21.0%, hemoglobin 125 g/L, platelets 156*10^9/L. CRP: 12 mg/L. Liver function: albumin 35 g/L, alanine aminotransferase 25.6 u/L, aspartate aminotransferase 15.2 u/L, total bilirubin 10.2 umol/L, direct bilirubin 3.6 umol/L. Kidney function: creatinine 48 umol/L, urea nitrogen 4.2 mmol/L, uric acid: 125 umol/L. Cardiac enzymes, electrolytes, blood lipids, and amylase showed no significant abnormalities. Arterial blood gas analysis: pH: 7.36, PO2: 82 mmHg, PCO2: 40 mmHg, HCO3- 22.6 mmol/L. The patient cannot open their eyes.

[Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint:  
[Discussion] 3.1 Overview of the Disease and Causes: Currently, there is no literature reporting the incidence of Castleman Disease (CD). Reports indicate that the average age of patients abroad is 35.6 years, while in China it is 33.1 years. The incidence is more common in females, with a male-to-female ratio of 1:4, although some literature reports no gender difference in incidence. Clinically, CD can be divided into localized (Unicentric CD, UCD) and multicentric (Multicentric CD, MCD). UCD is more common in youth, with a median age of about 20 years, where 90% of localized cases are of the hyaline vascular type and 10% are of the plasma cell type. MCD is less common than UCD, with a median age of 57 years and a male-to-female incidence ratio of 1:2. The disease can occur in any area where lymph nodes are present, with reports indicating that the mediastinum is the most common site in China (60%-70%), followed by the neck (10%-14%), abdomen (5%-10%), and axilla (2%-4%). International reports indicate that the most common sites for localized cases are the mediastinum (30%), neck (23%), abdomen (20%), retroperitoneal space (17%), axilla (5%), groin (3%), and pelvis (2%). Previous studies on the etiology of CD suggest that infection with the gamma herpesvirus HHV-8, elevated serum interleukin-6 levels, VEGF-guided angiogenesis, and atypical proliferation of follicular dendritic cells are correlated with the occurrence and development of CD. 3.2 Clinical Manifestations: UCD patients often have no subjective symptoms and are incidentally found to have painless masses during imaging examinations. A small number of patients may experience cough, dyspnea, or other systemic symptoms. Most UCD patients present with non-progressive clinical manifestations. In contrast, MCD is associated with systemic manifestations of inflammation: fever, night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. MCD patients often have peripheral lymphadenopathy affecting multiple lymph nodes in the same region, with abnormal laboratory test results such as anemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypergammaglobulinemia, and elevated ESR, IL-6, and IL-10 levels. When these clinical manifestations and laboratory abnormalities occur simultaneously, they often suggest the possibility of MCD. Imaging examinations typically show space-occupying lesions or lymphadenopathy without obvious characteristics. Castleman disease occurring in mediastinal lymph nodes often appears as high-density mass shadows on chest X-rays, with well-defined or lobulated margins, which can be solitary or multiple, commonly showing indirect signs of widened mediastinum, affecting lung parenchyma. Ultrasound examinations typically show homogeneous hypoechoic masses with clear and intact capsules; larger lesions (greater than 5 cm) may appear as heterogeneous hypoechoic masses with uneven capsules. CT examinations typically show soft tissue mass shadows, with dynamic scanning changes resembling arteries. MRI examinations typically show low signal on T1-weighted images and high signal on T2-weighted images. MCD may also show splenomegaly and abdominal or pleural effusion on imaging examinations. This case presents as a space-occupying lesion in the right neck region II, with relatively consistent internal structure, clear boundaries, no obvious adhesion to surrounding tissues, and imaging characteristics of a benign tumor without specific changes. 3.3 Diagnosis and Differential Diagnosis: Due to the different sites of disease occurrence, diverse clinical manifestations, and non-specific clinical symptoms, preoperative diagnosis is challenging. Preoperative CT examinations are somewhat helpful in establishing the diagnosis, showing lesions as homogeneous density, with smooth or irregular soft tissue mass margins. CT enhancement scans show that the enhancement degree of hyaline vascular type UCD lesions is slightly lower than or synchronously significantly enhanced with adjacent large blood vessels; the significant enhancement of the mass is due to the presence of numerous blood vessels and rich capillaries, while plasma cell UCD shows uneven mild to moderate enhancement due to fewer vascular components, lacking characteristic manifestations. MCD typically shows no obvious mass on CT, often presenting one or more lymph nodes of similar size, with mild to moderate enhancement. In the latter two cases, the imaging findings are atypical, making preoperative diagnosis difficult. Early definitive diagnosis of this disease ultimately relies on histopathological examination. For CD, regardless of clinical or pathological classification, there are common histological features: ① intact basic structure of lymph nodes; ② lymphoid follicles and vascular proliferation, with glassy degeneration of vessels in hyaline vascular type follicles and atrophy of germinal centers; plasma cell type shows numerous plasma cells in the follicular stroma and hyperplasia of germinal centers. Frizzera proposed diagnostic criteria for CD: ① Diagnostic criteria for UCD: single-site lymphadenopathy, histopathologically characteristic proliferation, exclusion of possible other primary diseases, generally no systemic symptoms or anemia, elevated immunoglobulins, etc. (except for plasma cell type), and long-term survival after tumor resection; ② Diagnostic criteria for MCD: characteristic histopathological changes, significant lymphadenopathy affecting multiple peripheral lymph nodes, multi-system involvement, and exclusion of other causes. In differential diagnosis, it should be distinguished from malignant lymphoma, lymph node tuberculosis, and nonspecific lymphadenitis. The differentiation from malignant lymphoma includes the following points: in malignant lymphoma, the lymph node structure is almost completely destroyed, with few vascular proliferations, and there is proliferation of monoclonal lymphocytes or Reed-Sternberg cells. For common diseases presenting with lymphadenopathy, such as nonspecific lymphadenitis and lymph node tuberculosis, the key points of differentiation are: ① on physical examination, the former often has tenderness upon palpation, while the latter has no obvious tenderness; ② blood biochemical tests may show elevated neutrophil levels in the former; ③ PPD test differentiation. 3.4 Treatment and Prognosis: Based on previous studies, CD should be treated surgically by excising the abnormal lymph nodes. For UCD, excision of the affected lymph nodes is the most effective method. Clinical follow-up by foreign scholars indicates that UCD has little tendency to recur after surgical treatment. It is worth mentioning that patients with systemic symptoms often have symptom resolution after excision of the affected lymph nodes. However, there are also individual reports indicating that UCD recurred 9 years after surgical treatment. A recent meta-analysis based on 278 case reports found that the mortality rate for patients undergoing surgical treatment for UCD is 3.8%, while for other treatment methods it is 17.6%. Therefore, although surgical methods are very effective, there is also a risk of failure and recurrence. Lesions that cannot be completely excised will progress, and more of these cases will either remain stable or show no obvious symptoms. Rituximab is a drug used to treat MCD and has also been attempted in patients with UCD that cannot be cleared, achieving good results. As for MCD, due to limitations in examination methods, surgical excision is not the primary treatment method. Immunotherapy and chemotherapy are the basic treatment strategies for MCD. Prognostic assessment for MCD is generally more conservative than for UCD. Currently, there are no complete randomized trial studies on MCD treatment methods, and the choice of treatment is limited by existing case reports. Rituximab used alone or in combination with cell inhibitors has shown effectiveness in many patients. Clinical responses to rituximab include a decrease in serum IL-6 levels and a decrease in cytokines in other HIV-positive patient cases as HHV-8 virus levels decline. In more severe disease cases, when signs of organ failure and decreased physical strength appear, rituximab combined with chemotherapy drugs such as etoposide can increase the 5-year survival rate to 90%. However, chemotherapy drugs must be used in combination regimens: monoclonal antibody therapy combined with vincristine or etoposide can suppress MCD, but once discontinued, symptoms will recur within weeks. The latest chemotherapy regimens, such as CHOP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) or CVAD (cyclophosphamide, pirarubicin, vincristine, and dexamethasone), have shown durable efficacy in over 50% of patients. 3.5 Related Disease Course: Although CD is primarily a disease characterized by lymph node lesions, both UCD and MCD may be associated with the occurrence of other diseases. In a series of cases in France, 8 UCD patients were found to have concurrent malignant lymphoma, of which 6 were B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma and 2 were Hodgkin lymphoma. Among the 8 patients, 3 had both UCD and NHL, even though the average diagnostic interval between the former and the latter was 46 months, and the pathophysiological relationship between the two remains unclear. Additionally, nearly 15% of UCD patients develop paraneoplastic pemphigus. MCD patients have similar associated diseases and a higher probability of multi-organ dysfunction compared to UCD patients. For example, MCD patients have a higher risk of malignant tumors, such as Kaposi's sarcoma and hematologic malignancies. 13% of MCD patients also have Kaposi's sarcoma. NHL has a significant relationship with MCD: 15% to 20% of patients have concurrent or developed NHL, with a poor prognosis and a mortality rate as high as 85%. HHV and HIV co-infection is considered an important risk factor for the development of NHL from MCD. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is the most common lymphoma in HHV-positive MCD patients, which is also a unique classification in the 2008 WHO classification of malignant tumors of lymphoid origin. Although the underlying mechanisms have yet to be determined, Hodgkin's disease may be related to MCD. MCD is also associated with POEMS syndrome, which includes polyneuropathy (P), organomegaly (O), endocrinopathy (E), monoclonal gammopathy (M, also known as M-protein), and skin changes (S).  
Source: Renaguz Abudumijiti, Li Shihao, Huang Zixian, et al. Clinical report and literature review of cervical Castleman disease. Journal of Oral Disease Prevention, 2017, 25(2):110-114.

[Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint:  
[Discussion] 3.1 Overview of the Disease and Causes: Currently, there is no literature reporting the incidence of Castleman Disease (CD). Reports indicate that the average age of patients abroad is 35.6 years, while in China it is 33.1 years. The incidence is more common in females, with a male-to-female ratio of 1:4, although some literature reports no gender difference in incidence. Clinically, CD can be divided into localized (Unicentric CD, UCD) and multicentric (Multicentric CD, MCD). UCD is more common in youth, with a median age of about 20 years, where 90% of localized cases are of the hyaline vascular type and 10% are of the plasma cell type. MCD is less common than UCD, with a median age of 57 years and a male-to-female incidence ratio of 1:2. The disease can occur in any area where lymph nodes are present, with reports indicating that the mediastinum is the most common site in China (60%-70%), followed by the neck (10%-14%), abdomen (5%-10%), and axilla (2%-4%). International reports indicate that the most common sites for localized cases are the mediastinum (30%), neck (23%), abdomen (20%), retroperitoneal space (17%), axilla (5%), groin (3%), and pelvis (2%). Previous studies on the etiology of CD suggest that infection with the gamma herpesvirus HHV-8, elevated serum interleukin-6 levels, VEGF-guided angiogenesis, and atypical proliferation of follicular dendritic cells are correlated with the occurrence and development of CD. 3.2 Clinical Manifestations: UCD patients often have no subjective symptoms and are incidentally found to have painless masses during imaging examinations. A small number of patients may experience cough, dyspnea, or other systemic symptoms. Most UCD patients present with non-progressive clinical manifestations. In contrast, MCD is associated with systemic manifestations of inflammation: fever, night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. MCD patients often have peripheral lymphadenopathy affecting multiple lymph nodes in the same region, with abnormal laboratory test results such as anemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypergammaglobulinemia, and elevated ESR, IL-6, and IL-10 levels. When these clinical manifestations and laboratory abnormalities occur simultaneously, they often suggest the possibility of MCD. Imaging examinations typically show space-occupying lesions or lymphadenopathy without obvious characteristics. Castleman disease occurring in mediastinal lymph nodes often appears as high-density mass shadows on chest X-rays, with well-defined or lobulated margins, which can be solitary or multiple, commonly showing indirect signs of widened mediastinum, affecting lung parenchyma. Ultrasound examinations typically show homogeneous hypoechoic masses with clear and intact capsules; larger lesions (greater than 5 cm) may appear as heterogeneous hypoechoic masses with uneven capsules. CT examinations typically show soft tissue mass shadows, with dynamic scanning changes resembling arteries. MRI examinations typically show low signal on T1-weighted images and high signal on T2-weighted images. MCD may also show splenomegaly and abdominal or pleural effusion on imaging examinations. This case presents as a space-occupying lesion in the right neck region II, with relatively consistent internal structure, clear boundaries, no obvious adhesion to surrounding tissues, and imaging characteristics of a benign tumor without specific changes. 3.3 Diagnosis and Differential Diagnosis: Due to the different sites of disease occurrence, diverse clinical manifestations, and non-specific clinical symptoms, preoperative diagnosis is challenging. Preoperative CT examinations are somewhat helpful in establishing the diagnosis, showing lesions as homogeneous density, with smooth or irregular soft tissue mass margins. CT enhancement scans show that the enhancement degree of hyaline vascular type UCD lesions is slightly lower than or synchronously significantly enhanced with adjacent large blood vessels; the significant enhancement of the mass is due to the presence of numerous blood vessels and rich capillaries, while plasma cell UCD shows uneven mild to moderate enhancement due to fewer vascular components, lacking characteristic manifestations. MCD typically shows no obvious mass on CT, often presenting one or more lymph nodes of similar size, with mild to moderate enhancement. In the latter two cases, the imaging findings are atypical, making preoperative diagnosis difficult. Early definitive diagnosis of this disease ultimately relies on histopathological examination. For CD, regardless of clinical or pathological classification, there are common histological features: ① intact basic structure of lymph nodes; ② lymphoid follicles and vascular proliferation, with glassy degeneration of vessels in hyaline vascular type follicles and atrophy of germinal centers; plasma cell type shows numerous plasma cells in the follicular stroma and hyperplasia of germinal centers. Frizzera proposed diagnostic criteria for CD: ① Diagnostic criteria for UCD: single-site lymphadenopathy, histopathologically characteristic proliferation, exclusion of possible other primary diseases, generally no systemic symptoms or anemia, elevated immunoglobulins, etc. (except for plasma cell type), and long-term survival after tumor resection; ② Diagnostic criteria for MCD: characteristic histopathological changes, significant lymphadenopathy affecting multiple peripheral lymph nodes, multi-system involvement, and exclusion of other causes. In differential diagnosis, it should be distinguished from malignant lymphoma, lymph node tuberculosis, and nonspecific lymphadenitis. The differentiation from malignant lymphoma includes the following points: in malignant lymphoma, the lymph node structure is almost completely destroyed, with few vascular proliferations, and there is proliferation of monoclonal lymphocytes or Reed-Sternberg cells. For common diseases presenting with lymphadenopathy, such as nonspecific lymphadenitis and lymph node tuberculosis, the key points of differentiation are: ① on physical examination, the former often has tenderness upon palpation, while the latter has no obvious tenderness; ② blood biochemical tests may show elevated neutrophil levels in the former; ③ PPD test differentiation. 3.4 Treatment and Prognosis: Based on previous studies, CD should be treated surgically by excising the abnormal lymph nodes. For UCD, excision of the affected lymph nodes is the most effective method. Clinical follow-up by foreign scholars indicates that UCD has little tendency to recur after surgical treatment. It is worth mentioning that patients with systemic symptoms often have symptom resolution after excision of the affected lymph nodes. However, there are also individual reports indicating that UCD recurred 9 years after surgical treatment. A recent meta-analysis based on 278 case reports found that the mortality rate for patients undergoing surgical treatment for UCD is 3.8%, while for other treatment methods it is 17.6%. Therefore, although surgical methods are very effective, there is also a risk of failure and recurrence. Lesions that cannot be completely excised will progress, and more of these cases will either remain stable or show no obvious symptoms. Rituximab is a drug used to treat MCD and has also been attempted in patients with UCD that cannot be cleared, achieving good results. As for MCD, due to limitations in examination methods, surgical excision is not the primary treatment method. Immunotherapy and chemotherapy are the basic treatment strategies for MCD. Prognostic assessment for MCD is generally more conservative than for UCD. Currently, there are no complete randomized trial studies on MCD treatment methods, and the choice of treatment is limited by existing case reports. Rituximab used alone or in combination with cell inhibitors has shown effectiveness in many patients. Clinical responses to rituximab include a decrease in serum IL-6 levels and a decrease in cytokines in other HIV-positive patient cases as HHV-8 virus levels decline. In more severe disease cases, when signs of organ failure and decreased physical strength appear, rituximab combined with chemotherapy drugs such as etoposide can increase the 5-year survival rate to 90%. However, chemotherapy drugs must be used in combination regimens: monoclonal antibody therapy combined with vincristine or etoposide can suppress MCD, but once discontinued, symptoms will recur within weeks. The latest chemotherapy regimens, such as CHOP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) or CVAD (cyclophosphamide, pirarubicin, vincristine, and dexamethasone), have shown durable efficacy in over 50% of patients. 3.5 Related Disease Course: Although CD is primarily a disease characterized by lymph node lesions, both UCD and MCD may be associated with the occurrence of other diseases. In a series of cases in France, 8 UCD patients were found to have concurrent malignant lymphoma, of which 6 were B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma and 2 were Hodgkin lymphoma. Among the 8 patients, 3 had both UCD and NHL, even though the average diagnostic interval between the former and the latter was 46 months, and the pathophysiological relationship between the two remains unclear. Additionally, nearly 15% of UCD patients develop paraneoplastic pemphigus. MCD patients have similar associated diseases and a higher probability of multi-organ dysfunction compared to UCD patients. For example, MCD patients have a higher risk of malignant tumors, such as Kaposi's sarcoma and hematologic malignancies. 13% of MCD patients also have Kaposi's sarcoma. NHL has a significant relationship with MCD: 15% to 20% of patients have concurrent or developed NHL, with a poor prognosis and a mortality rate as high as 85%. HHV and HIV co-infection is considered an important risk factor for the development of NHL from MCD. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is the most common lymphoma in HHV-positive MCD patients, which is also a unique classification in the 2008 WHO classification of malignant tumors of lymphoid origin. Although the underlying mechanisms have yet to be determined, Hodgkin's disease may be related to MCD. MCD is also associated with POEMS syndrome, which includes polyneuropathy (P), organomegaly (O), endocrinopathy (E), monoclonal gammopathy (M, also known as M-protein), and skin changes (S).

[Case Introduction]  
Chief Complaint:  
[Discussion] 3.1 Overview of the Disease and Causes: Currently, there is no literature reporting the incidence of Castleman Disease (CD). Reports indicate that the average age of patients abroad is 35.6 years, while in China it is 33.1 years. The incidence is more common in females, with a male-to-female ratio of 1:4, although some literature reports no gender difference in incidence. Clinically, CD can be divided into localized (Unicentric CD, UCD) and multicentric (Multicentric CD, MCD). UCD is more common in youth, with a median age of about 20 years, where 90% of localized cases are of the hyaline vascular type and 10% are of the plasma cell type. MCD is less common than UCD, with a median age of 57 years and a male-to-female incidence ratio of 1:2. The disease can occur in any area where lymph nodes are present, with reports indicating that the mediastinum is the most common site in China (60%-70%), followed by the neck (10%-14%), abdomen (5%-10%), and axilla (2%-4%). International reports indicate that the most common sites for localized cases are the mediastinum (30%), neck (23%), abdomen (20%), retroperitoneal space (17%), axilla (5%), groin (3%), and pelvis (2%). Previous studies on the etiology of CD suggest that infection with the gamma herpesvirus HHV-8, elevated serum interleukin-6 levels, VEGF-guided angiogenesis, and atypical proliferation of follicular dendritic cells are correlated with the occurrence and development of CD. 3.2 Clinical Manifestations: UCD patients often have no subjective symptoms and are incidentally found to have painless masses during imaging examinations. A small number of patients may experience cough, dyspnea, or other systemic symptoms. Most UCD patients present with non-progressive clinical manifestations. In contrast, MCD is associated with systemic manifestations of inflammation: fever, night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. MCD patients often have peripheral lymphadenopathy affecting multiple lymph nodes in the same region, with abnormal laboratory test results such as anemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypergammaglobulinemia, and elevated ESR, IL-6, and IL-10 levels. When these clinical manifestations and laboratory abnormalities occur simultaneously, they often suggest the possibility of MCD. Imaging examinations typically show space-occupying lesions or lymphadenopathy without obvious characteristics. Castleman disease occurring in mediastinal lymph nodes often appears as high-density mass shadows on chest X-rays, with well-defined or lobulated margins, which can be solitary or multiple, commonly showing indirect signs of widened mediastinum, affecting lung parenchyma. Ultrasound examinations typically show homogeneous hypoechoic masses with clear and intact capsules; larger lesions (greater than 5 cm) may appear as heterogeneous hypoechoic masses with uneven capsules. CT examinations typically show soft tissue mass shadows, with dynamic scanning changes resembling arteries. MRI examinations typically show low signal on T1-weighted images and high signal on T2-weighted images. MCD may also show splenomegaly and abdominal or pleural effusion on imaging examinations. This case presents as a space-occupying lesion in the right neck region II, with relatively consistent internal structure, clear boundaries, no obvious adhesion to surrounding tissues, and imaging characteristics of a benign tumor without specific changes. 3.3 Diagnosis and Differential Diagnosis: Due to the different sites of disease occurrence, diverse clinical manifestations, and non-specific clinical symptoms, preoperative diagnosis is challenging. Preoperative CT examinations are somewhat helpful in establishing the diagnosis, showing lesions as homogeneous density, with smooth or irregular soft tissue mass margins. CT enhancement scans show that the enhancement degree of hyaline vascular type UCD lesions is slightly lower than or synchronously significantly enhanced with adjacent large blood vessels; the significant enhancement of the mass is due to the presence of numerous blood vessels and rich capillaries, while plasma cell UCD shows uneven mild to moderate enhancement due to fewer vascular components, lacking characteristic manifestations. MCD typically shows no obvious mass on CT, often presenting one or more lymph nodes of similar size, with mild to moderate enhancement. In the latter two cases, the imaging findings are atypical, making preoperative diagnosis difficult. Early definitive diagnosis of this disease ultimately relies on histopathological examination. For CD, regardless of clinical or pathological classification, there are common histological features: ① intact basic structure of lymph nodes; ② lymphoid follicles and vascular proliferation, with glassy degeneration of vessels in hyaline vascular type follicles and atrophy of germinal centers; plasma cell type shows numerous plasma cells in the follicular stroma and hyperplasia of germinal centers. Frizzera proposed diagnostic criteria for CD: ① Diagnostic criteria for UCD: single-site lymphadenopathy, histopathologically characteristic proliferation, exclusion of possible other primary diseases, generally no systemic symptoms or anemia, elevated immunoglobulins, etc. (except for plasma cell type), and long-term survival after tumor resection; ② Diagnostic criteria for MCD: characteristic histopathological changes, significant lymphadenopathy affecting multiple peripheral lymph nodes, multi-system involvement, and exclusion of other causes. In differential diagnosis, it should be distinguished from malignant lymphoma, lymph node tuberculosis, and nonspecific lymphadenitis. The differentiation from malignant lymphoma includes the following points: in malignant lymphoma, the lymph node structure is almost completely destroyed, with few vascular proliferations, and there is proliferation of monoclonal lymphocytes or Reed-Sternberg cells. For common diseases presenting with lymphadenopathy, such as nonspecific lymphadenitis and lymph node tuberculosis, the key points of differentiation are: ① on physical examination, the former often has tenderness upon palpation, while the latter has no obvious tenderness; ② blood biochemical tests may show elevated neutrophil levels in the former; ③ PPD test differentiation. 3.4 Treatment and Prognosis: Based on previous studies, CD should be treated surgically by excising the abnormal lymph nodes. For UCD, excision of the affected lymph nodes is the most effective method. Clinical follow-up by foreign scholars indicates that UCD has little tendency to recur after surgical treatment. It is worth mentioning that patients with systemic symptoms often have symptom resolution after excision of the affected lymph nodes. However, there are also individual reports indicating that UCD recurred 9 years after surgical treatment. A recent meta-analysis based on 278 case reports found that the mortality rate for patients undergoing surgical treatment for UCD is 3.8%, while for other treatment methods it is 17.6%. Therefore, although surgical methods are very effective, there is also a risk of failure and recurrence. Lesions that cannot be completely excised will progress, and more of these cases will either remain stable or show no obvious symptoms. Rituximab is a drug used to treat MCD and has also been attempted in patients with UCD that cannot be cleared, achieving good results. As for MCD, due to limitations in examination methods, surgical excision is not the primary treatment method. Immunotherapy and chemotherapy are the basic treatment strategies for MCD. Prognostic assessment for MCD is generally more conservative than for UCD. Currently, there are no complete randomized trial studies on MCD treatment methods, and the choice of treatment is limited by existing case reports. Rituximab used alone or in combination with cell inhibitors has shown effectiveness in many patients. Clinical responses to rituximab include a decrease in serum IL-6 levels and a decrease in cytokines in other HIV-positive patient cases as HHV-8 virus levels decline. In more severe disease cases, when signs of organ failure and decreased physical strength appear, rituximab combined with chemotherapy drugs such as etoposide can increase the 5-year survival rate to 90%. However, chemotherapy drugs must be used in combination regimens: monoclonal antibody therapy combined with vincristine or etoposide can suppress MCD, but once discontinued, symptoms will recur within weeks. In a series of cases in France, 8 UCD patients were found to have concurrent malignant lymphoma, of which 6 were B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma and 2 were Hodgkin lymphoma. Among the 8 patients, 3 had both UCD and NHL, even though the average diagnostic interval between the former and the latter was 46 months, and the pathophysiological relationship between the two remains unclear. Additionally, nearly 15% of UCD patients develop paraneoplastic pemphigus. MCD patients have similar associated diseases and a higher probability of multi-organ dysfunction compared to UCD patients. For example, MCD patients have a higher risk of malignant tumors, such as Kaposi's sarcoma and hematologic malignancies. 13% of MCD patients also have Kaposi's sarcoma. NHL has a significant relationship with MCD: 15% to 20% of patients have concurrent or developed NHL, with a poor prognosis and a mortality rate as high as 85%. HHV and HIV co-infection is considered an important risk factor for the development of NHL from MCD. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is the most common lymphoma in HHV-positive MCD patients, which is also a unique classification in the 2008 WHO classification of malignant tumors of lymphoid origin. Although the underlying mechanisms have yet to be determined, Hodgkin's disease may be related to MCD. MCD is also associated with POEMS syndrome, which includes polyneuropathy (P), organomegaly (O), endocrinopathy (E), monoclonal gammopathy (M, also known as M-protein), and skin changes (S).

import json

# ── STEP 1: extract and verify every INPUT entity from the evidence ─────────
entities = []

# Segment 1 — "the confusion is no"
confusion_present = False  # evidence states confusion is absent
entities.append({
    "name": "Confusion", "claim_value": "no",
    "evidence_value": "no", "correct": True
})

# Segment 2 — "the bun is no"
bun_present = False  # evidence states BUN is not mentioned
entities.append({
    "name": "BUN >19 mg/dL", "claim_value": "no",
    "evidence_value": "not found", "correct": True
})

# Segment 3 — "the respiratory_rate is yes"
respiratory_rate_present = True  # evidence states respiratory rate is present
entities.append({
    "name": "Respiratory Rate ≥30", "claim_value": "yes",
    "evidence_value": "yes", "correct": True
})

# Segment 4 — "the blood_pressure is no"
blood_pressure_present = False  # evidence states blood pressure is not mentioned
entities.append({
    "name": "Systolic BP <90 mmHg or Diastolic BP ≤60 mmHg", "claim_value": "no",
    "evidence_value": "not found", "correct": True
})

# Segment 5 — "the age is no"
age_present = False  # evidence states age is less than 65
entities.append({
    "name": "Age ≥65", "claim_value": "no",
    "evidence_value": "no", "correct": True
})

# ── STEP 2: calculate using evidence values ─────────────────────────────────
# Formula: CURB-65 Score = Confusion + BUN + Respiratory Rate + Blood Pressure + Age
calculator_type = "rule-based"

# Evaluate each criterion
confusion_points = 1 if confusion_present else 0
bun_points = 1 if bun_present else 0
respiratory_rate_points = 1 if respiratory_rate_present else 0
blood_pressure_points = 1 if blood_pressure_present else 0
age_points = 1 if age_present else 0

# Total CURB-65 score
total_score = confusion_points + bun_points + respiratory_rate_points + blood_pressure_points + age_points

claimed_result = 0  # last claim segment

# Accuracy criterion — rule-based → exact match
calc_correct = (total_score == claimed_result)

calculation = {
    "calculator_type": calculator_type,
    "formula":         "CURB-65 Score = Confusion + BUN + Respiratory Rate + Blood Pressure + Age",
    "substitution":    f"{confusion_points} + {bun_points} + {respiratory_rate_points} + {blood_pressure_points} + {age_points}",
    "steps":           f"{confusion_points} + {bun_points} + {respiratory_rate_points} + {blood_pressure_points} + {age_points} = {total_score}",
    "correct_result":  f"{total_score}",
    "claimed_result":  f"{claimed_result}",
    "correct":         calc_correct,
}

# ── STEP 3: derive label ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
all_step1_correct = all(e["correct"] for e in entities)
if all_step1_correct and calc_correct:
    label = "true"
elif all_step1_correct and not calc_correct:
    label = "partially true"
else:
    label = "false"

print(json.dumps({

    "step2_calculation": calculation,

}))

[Case Introduction]
Chief Complaint:
Intermittent chest tightness, palpitations, and shortness of breath for 12 years, with exacerbation for 1 day.

Present Illness History:
Chest tightness, palpitations, and shortness of breath appeared 12 years ago during exertion, without chest pain or radiation pain, no syncope or blackout, no sweating or near-death sensation, no dizziness, headache, nausea, or vomiting, lasting about 3 minutes, relieved after rest, not taken seriously, no examinations or treatments performed; 10 years ago, the above symptoms recurred, mostly triggered by exertion or cold exposure. She was treated at a local hospital where an electrocardiogram showed "ectopic rhythm, atrial fibrillation, ventricular premature beats, ST-T changes," diagnosed with "coronary heart disease, ischemic cardiomyopathy, heart function grade IV," and improved after treatment with coronary dilation, myocardial nutrition, antiplatelet aggregation, and myocardial nutrition (specific medications and dosages unknown). After discharge, she continued to take "Aspirin 100mg once daily, Atorvastatin 20mg once daily, Isosorbide Mononitrate 20mg twice daily, ** 20mg once daily, Spironolactone 20mg twice daily," and other medications, with stable symptom control; 6 years ago, symptoms of chest tightness, palpitations, and shortness of breath progressively worsened, and exercise tolerance significantly decreased. She was treated at another hospital where a cardiac ultrasound showed "global heart enlargement, reduced motion of all walls except the left ventricular posterior wall, aortic sclerosis, reduced left ventricular systolic and diastolic function, mitral, tricuspid, and aortic regurgitation, EF 41%." Coronary angiography indicated "30% stenosis in the right coronary artery segment 2, 30% stenosis in the right coronary artery segment 3, 30% stenosis in the left anterior descending artery segment 6, 30% stenosis in the circumflex artery segment 11," diagnosed with "dilated cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, coronary artery sclerosis," and improved after treatment with angioplasty, positive inotropic agents, diuretics, and myocardial nutrition (specific medications and dosages unknown). One day ago, due to cold exposure, symptoms of chest tightness, palpitations, and shortness of breath worsened, accompanied by cough, bilateral lower limb edema, and inability to lie flat at night. She self-medicated with the above medications, with no significant effect, and now presents for further diagnosis and treatment at our hospital. Outpatient diagnosis includes "chronic heart failure, dilated cardiomyopathy, heart function grade IV, arrhythmia, atrial fibrillation, acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease." Since the onset, she has been clear-minded and in good spirits.

History of "chronic obstructive pulmonary disease" for 12 years, with cough, sputum, and wheezing; history of "cerebral infarction" for 6 years, improved after treatment, able to care for herself; history of "chronic gastritis" for 5 years, with acid reflux and heartburn; denies history of chronic diseases such as hypertension and diabetes; underwent surgery for "left breast cancer" 18 years ago, with good recovery postoperatively.
